 {"id":"bd-38k","title":"Implement SSI write-skew detection with rw-antidependency tracking","description":"# Implement SSI write-skew detection with rw-antidependency tracking

## GOAL
Implement Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) to detect write-skew anomalies that FCW alone cannot catch.

## BACKGROUND (Alien-Artifact Quality)
FCW detects write-write conflicts. But write-skew is possible:

```
T1: reads A, writes B
T2: reads B, writes A
Both commit under SI -> non-serializable!
```

SSI (Cahill-Rohm-Fekete 2008) detects this via rw-antidependency cycles.

## FORMAL MODEL

### Definitions
- **rw-antidependency T1 -rw-> T2**: T1 read block B at version V, T2 later wrote V for B
- **Dangerous structure**: Consecutive rw-antideps forming a cycle

### Detection Algorithm
At commit time for transaction T:
1. For each block B in T.write_set:
   - Find all transactions T that read B at earlier version
   - If any such T is concurrent and has rw-dep on T -> dangerous
2. For each block B in T.read_set:
   - If committed transaction T wrote B after T read it -> T has rw-dep
3. If T participates in a dangerous structure -> abort

### Implementation
```rust
pub struct SsiTracker {
    /// Active transactions and their read/write sets
    active: BTreeMap<TxnId, TxnSets>,
    /// Recent commits for rw-dep detection
    recent_commits: VecDeque<CommittedTxn>,
}

impl SsiTracker {
    pub fn check_commit(&self, txn: &Transaction) -> Result<(), SsiConflict>;
    pub fn record_commit(&mut self, txn_id: TxnId, write_set: &BTreeSet<BlockNumber>);
    pub fn prune_old(&mut self, watermark: CommitSeq);
}
```

## TESTS
1. Unit: Write-skew detected and aborted
2. Unit: Non-conflicting concurrent txns commit
3. Integration: Classic write-skew scenario fails
4. Lab: Deterministic interleaving tests

## LOGGING
- SSI conflict (warn): txn_ids involved, blocks
- Dangerous structure (debug): cycle description

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Write-skew anomalies impossible
2. False positive rate < 1% for non-conflicting workloads
3. Overhead < 5% vs FCW-only
4.  {"id":"bd-5s0r","title":"EPIC: Write-Back Cache","description":"# EPIC: Write-Back Cache

## PURPOSE
Implement a write-back cache layer between FUSE write operations and disk I/O. This is critical for write performance — without write-back caching, every FUSE write would require immediate disk I/O (write-through), making FrankenFS orders of magnitude slower than kernel filesystems.

## DESIGN

### Phased Approach
1. DirtyTracker: track which cached blocks have been modified but not flushed
2. FlushDaemon: background thread that periodically flushes dirty blocks to disk
3. MVCC Integration: dirty block tracking integrates with MVCC version chains

### Core Invariant
**Dirty blocks must never be evicted from cache until flushed to disk.**
Violating this invariant causes data loss (dirty data discarded without writing).

### DirtyTracker
- Per-block dirty flag in cache metadata
- Dirty list: ordered by age (oldest dirty block first)
- Statistics: dirty block count, dirty data size, oldest dirty age

### FlushDaemon
- Background thread wakes periodically (configurable: default 5s)
- Flushes oldest dirty blocks first (LRU-like)
- Flush batch size: configurable (default 256 blocks)
- Integrates with fsync: explicit fsync flushes all dirty blocks for that inode
- Backpressure: if dirty ratio exceeds threshold (e.g., 80%), block new writes until flushed

### MVCC Integration
- Dirty blocks belong to a specific MVCC version
- Flush writes the committed version (not in-flight versions)
- Aborted transaction: dirty blocks discarded without flush
- Committed transaction: dirty blocks eligible for flush

### Eviction Coordination
- Cache eviction (ARC or S3-FIFO) must check dirty flag before evicting
- If dirty: flush first, then evict
- If flush fails: do NOT evict (keep dirty block in cache, retry later)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Dirty blocks tracked correctly (set on write, cleared on flush)
- [ ] FlushDaemon runs in background, flushes oldest dirty blocks
- [ ] fsync flushes all dirty blocks for specified inode
- [ ] Dirty blocks never evicted without flush (invariant enforced)
- [ ] Backpressure prevents OOM from too many dirty blocks
- [ ] MVCC integration: only committed versions flushed
- [ ] Benchmark: write throughput with write-back vs write-through

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E durability test (bd-5s0r.4) passes
3. Dirty blocks never evicted without flush (invariant enforced)
4. Write throughput >= 80pct of kernel ext4 write-back
5. fsync flushes all dirty blocks for target inode","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:26:02.706174558Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T23:46:05.116910898Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T23:46:05.116892123Z","close_reason":"All children closed.  {"id":"bd-cebm","title":"Implement FUSE fsync/flush with durability guarantees","description":"# Implement FUSE fsync/flush with durability guarantees

## GOAL
Implement correct fsync and flush semantics for FUSE mount, ensuring durability guarantees match user expectations.

## BACKGROUND
fsync is the primary durability contract:
- `fsync(fd)` must ensure all data written to fd is on stable storage
- `fdatasync(fd)` same but may skip non-essential metadata
- `flush` is called on close (cleanup, not durability)

## CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. fsync Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn fsync(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, datasync: bool, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // 1. Get inode from fh
        // 2. Commit any pending MVCC transaction for this file
        // 3. Flush dirty cache entries for this inode's blocks
        // 4. If !datasync: also flush inode metadata block
        // 5. Wait for I/O completion
        // 6. If repair_eager: refresh RaptorQ symbols for dirty groups
        // 7. reply.ok() or reply.error(errno)
    }
}
```

### 2. Flush Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn flush(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, lock_owner: u64, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // Called on close - release resources, no durability guarantee
        // 1. Release file handle
        // 2. Decrement open count
        // 3. If last close + unlinked: queue for deletion
        // 4. reply.ok()
    }
}
```

### 3. fsyncdir Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn fsyncdir(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, datasync: bool, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // Same as fsync but for directories
        // Ensures directory entries are durable
    }
}
```

### 4. Integration Points
- **MVCC**: fsync triggers transaction commit
- **ARC Cache**: fsync flushes dirty entries for inode
- **RaptorQ**: fsync may trigger eager symbol refresh
- **Journal**: fsync ensures journal commit block written

### 5. Error Handling
- I/O error during fsync -> EROFS or EIO
- MVCC conflict -> EAGAIN (retry)
- Timeout -> EIO with detailed logging

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: fsync flushes dirty blocks for inode
2. Unit: fdatasync skips metadata
3. Integration: fsync + crash + recovery -> data durable
4. Integration: fsync on RO mount -> EROFS
5. E2E: Write + fsync + unmount + remount -> data present

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- fsync start (debug): inode, datasync flag
- fsync cache flush (trace): block count, dirty bytes
- fsync commit (trace): commit_seq
- fsync complete (debug): duration_us, blocks_flushed
- fsync error (warn): inode, error, context

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. fsync guarantees data is on stable storage
2. fsync failure returns correct errno
3. No data loss if process crashes after successful fsync
4. +{"id":"bd-h9lpq","title":"[HIGH] 16 KiB btrfs readdir fixture bypasses the shared checksum helper and drifts from parser semantics","description":"# Review finding

The remaining red btrfs directory-walk family is the only synthetic-tree fixture in `ffs-core` still hand-rolling tree-block checksum logic instead of going through the shared test helper.

## Evidence
- Shared helper exists at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22328-22332`: `stamp_btrfs_test_tree_block_crc32c(image, logical)`.
- That helper is used by the green 4 KiB synthetic-tree fixtures (`crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22357-22561`, `23010-23011`).
- `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` instead inlines its own checksum math at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23372-23375` while also switching the superblock `nodesize` to `16384`.
- The current full workspace gate still fails the six readdir-specific tests on `tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)`, which means the hand-rolled 16 KiB path has drifted from what the parser expects.

## Why this is high severity
This is now the only remaining workspace-red btrfs fixture family after the default-root smoke fix landed. Keeping a second checksum implementation in test builders is actively hiding the real contract and slowing down recovery.

## Expected fix direction
Generalize the shared test checksum helper to accept nodesize/logical span explicitly, then route `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` through that helper and add a narrow regression test that proves a 16 KiB stamped leaf round-trips through the current parser.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","created_by":"cod-fs","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","ffs-core","fixtures","qa","review","workspace-gate"]}

 {"id":"bd-idp4","title":"Update CHANGELOG.md to cover commits from 2026-03-21 through current","description":"## What
Update CHANGELOG.md to document all significant commits since the last entry (2026-03-21).
Approximately 60+ commits need documentation, organized by capability area (not chronological).

## Why
CHANGELOG.md covers "2026-02-09 through 2026-03-21" with 536 commits documented. Since then,
major work has landed: btrfs experimental RW support, crash-consistency matrix, fallocate
operations, log contract hardening, per-core FUSE dispatch, evidence pipeline expansion,
tracing flake fixes, and 81+ new tests across 4 crates.

The CHANGELOG serves as the project history for onboarding and as input to the
comprehensive-codebase-report skill. Stale changelog = invisible work.

## Implementation Plan
1. Run: git log --oneline --since="2026-03-21" | wc -l (count commits)
2. Group commits by capability area (following existing CHANGELOG structure):
   - On-Disk Parsing (ext4/btrfs)
   - FUSE Mount and VFS Layer
   - MVCC Concurrency Engine
   - Self-Healing Repair Pipeline
   - Write Path (Allocation, Extents, Inodes, Directories, Xattrs)
   - Journal and WAL Recovery
   - CLI and Observability
   - Conformance Harness and Testing
   - Foundation Types and Error Handling
3. Write entries with commit links: [hexsha](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenfs/commit/hexsha)
4. Update header: "Period covered: 2026-02-09 through 2026-04-06"
5. Update total commit count

## Considerations
- Follow existing style: bullet points grouped by feature area, commit links in brackets
- Don't document individual test additions — summarize as "expanded test coverage for X"
- Include the tracing callsite cache fix as it's architecturally interesting
- Include the btrfs fallocate sector-alignment fix
- Include the per-core metrics test fix
- Don't document build/fmt/cosmetic commits

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All significant commits since 2026-03-21 documented
- [ ] Organized by capability area matching existing structure
- [ ] Commit links included and correct

-{"id":"bd-cb0kd","title":"[HIGH] build_btrfs_readdir_image still computes invalid tree_block_csum after explicit stamping","description":"# Review finding

The current `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` fixture already writes explicit CRC32C values into both synthetic tree blocks, but `rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenfs_cod cargo test --workspace --all-targets` still fails the six btrfs directory-walk tests with `Format("invalid field: tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)")`.

## Evidence
- Builder already stamps both blocks: `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23225-23378`.
- It computes `csum_root` and `csum_fs` over `image[tree + 0x20 .. tree + nodesize]` and writes them to bytes `0..4`.
- After `br-bd-o5114 repair btrfs default-root smoke fixture`, the remaining red frontier is exactly:
  - `walk_btrfs_dir_entry_items_includes_dir_index_items`
  - `btrfs_readdir_mixed_types`
  - `btrfs_readdir_empty_directory_returns_dot_dotdot`
  - `btrfs_readdir_sorted_by_index`
  - `btrfs_readdir_offset_pagination`
  - `btrfs_readdir_special_characters_in_names`
- All six still fail on the same checksum mismatch, so the root cause is no longer "missing stamp calls".

## Why this is high severity
This keeps the full workspace test gate red and shows the older broad review bead is now under-specified. The next implementation pass needs to debug the actual checksum semantics, not just add stamp writes.

## Expected fix direction
Audit the exact checksum window, node header fields, and final byte mutations for the 16 KiB synthetic readdir fixture. Verify the bytes being hashed match what the parser later validates.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","created_by":"cod-fs","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","ffs-core","fixtures","qa","review","workspace-gate"]}
-{"id":"bd-h9lpq","title":"[HIGH] 16 KiB btrfs readdir fixture bypasses the shared checksum helper and drifts from parser semantics","description":"# Review finding

The remaining red btrfs directory-walk family is the only synthetic-tree fixture in `ffs-core` still hand-rolling tree-block checksum logic instead of going through the shared test helper.

## Evidence
- Shared helper exists at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22328-22332`: `stamp_btrfs_test_tree_block_crc32c(image, logical)`.
- That helper is used by the green 4 KiB synthetic-tree fixtures (`crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22357-22561`, `23010-23011`).
- `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` instead inlines its own checksum math at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23372-23375` while also switching the superblock `nodesize` to `16384`.
- The current full workspace gate still fails the six readdir-specific tests on `tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)`, which means the hand-rolled 16 KiB path has drifted from what the parser expects.

## Why this is high severity
This is now the only remaining workspace-red btrfs fixture family after the default-root smoke fix landed. Keeping a second checksum implementation in test builders is actively hiding the real contract and slowing down recovery.

## Expected fix direction
Generalize the shared test checksum helper to accept nodesize/logical span explicitly, then route `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` through that helper and add a narrow regression test that proves a 16 KiB stamped leaf round-trips through the current parser.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","created_by":"cod-fs","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:53Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","ffs-core","fixtures","qa","review","workspace-gate"]}
